title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
Identify key diagnostic features of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
Apply clinical reasoning to Abnormal Uterine Bleeding management
Recognize common pitfalls in Abnormal Uterine Bleeding

sections.title: Overview of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
sections.content:
**Abnormal Uterine Bleeding**

PALM-COEIN classification, structural vs non-structural causes, workup approach, management

**Key Points:**
• PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy — Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
• First step: pregnancy test (always rule out pregnancy in reproductive-age women with AUB)
• Structural causes (PALM): ultrasound as first-line imaging

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy — Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
• First step: pregnancy test (always rule out pregnancy in reproductive-age women with AUB)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Structural causes (PALM): ultrasound as first-line imaging
• Ovulatory dysfunction: most common cause of AUB in adolescents and perimenopausal women (anovulatory cycles → unopposed estrogen)
• Medical management: hormonal (OCPs, levonorgestrel IUD, progestins) or non-hormonal (tranexamic acid, NSAIDs)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Abnormal Uterine Bleeding:**

• Structural causes (PALM): ultrasound as first-line imaging
• Ovulatory dysfunction: most common cause of AUB in adolescents and perimenopausal women (anovulatory cycles → unopposed estrogen)
• Medical management: hormonal (OCPs, levonorgestrel IUD, progestins) or non-hormonal (tranexamic acid, NSAIDs)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding?

options.text: PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy — Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
options.explanation: PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy — Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Abnormal Uterine Bleeding?

options.text: Medical management: hormonal (OCPs, levonorgestrel IUD, progestins) or non-hormonal (tranexamic acid, NSAIDs)
options.explanation: Medical management: hormonal (OCPs, levonorgestrel IUD, progestins) or non-hormonal (tranexamic acid, NSAIDs)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Abnormal Uterine Bleeding. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: First step: pregnancy test (always rule out pregnancy in reproductive-age women with AUB)
options.explanation: First step: pregnancy test (always rule out pregnancy in reproductive-age women with AUB)

mcqs.stem: A G2P1 at 38 weeks presents with painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound shows a complete placenta previa. What is the recommended management?

options.text: Scheduled cesarean delivery at 36-37 weeks if stable; emergent C-section if hemorrhaging
options.explanation: Per ACOG, complete placenta previa is an absolute contraindication to vaginal delivery due to risk of life-threatening hemorrhage. If asymptomatic, planned cesarean at 36-37+6 weeks is recommended. Active hemorrhage at any gestational age requires emergent cesarean. At 38 weeks with bleeding, proceed to delivery.

options.text: Attempt vaginal delivery with close monitoring
options.explanation: Vaginal delivery is absolutely contraindicated with complete placenta previa — the placenta completely covers the cervical os, and cervical dilation would cause catastrophic hemorrhage.

options.text: Expectant management with bed rest until 40 weeks
options.explanation: At 38 weeks with active bleeding and complete previa, expectant management risks maternal hemorrhage. Delivery is indicated.

options.text: Digital cervical exam to assess dilation
options.explanation: Digital cervical exam is CONTRAINDICATED in placenta previa — it can disrupt the placenta and precipitate massive hemorrhage. Always confirm placental location by ultrasound before any cervical examination in third-trimester bleeding.
